currentState: In chapter 23 the relationship resolves into direct predator-counterpredator combat inside the throne room. Sylvanas treats Arthas as a target whose body only matters once Frostmourne’s protection breaks, and Arthas treats Sylvanas as one of the two intrusions capable of turning the room against him.
powerDynamic: Arthas owns the chamber so long as Frostmourne’s rhythm stays intact. Sylvanas owns movement, angle theft, and the final shot once Jaina’s work makes ordinary trajectory matter again. The dynamic ends only with his death.

physicalDynamics.pattern: Sylvanas brings her horse level and a fraction ahead beside Jaina, signaling to Arthas's camp that the warning arrives under her command as well as Jaina's knowledge.
Their exchange never warms: his glance measures Farstrider leathers and her face; her stillness answers the room with refusal before the argument even begins.
Inside the pavilion Sylvanas stands as the only outside commander who can redraw Arthas’s map in real time, while Arthas physically takes the table the instant the pattern locks.
Their final exchange stays cold and clean: invitation, refusal, acceptance, separation.
The chapter stages them over the same table but looking toward different consequences: Arthas pressing the map forward, Sylvanas reading the cost hidden in his momentum.
They share the same gate only briefly before Arthas turns inward toward the city and Sylvanas turns sideways toward witness and survival.
The last meaningful body language between them is vertical rather than conversational: Arthas below the wall looking up, Sylvanas above it refusing to move.
The key chapter-21 image is Sylvanas taking the first clean shot and learning from the failure faster than anyone else in the room. The relationship becomes defined by that instant recalculation.
Their confrontation is brief and brutally physical: bowline, broken angle, ice-shard violence, Muradin falling, Sylvanas shifting from engagement to extraction before pride can get people buried.
Chapter 23 is close-range pursuit geometry: Sylvanas drawing Arthas away from Jaina, crossing columns, hitting stone, rising again, and making the room cost him attention he cannot spare.
Their last exchange is pure ballistic fact. Jaina opens the seam; Sylvanas shoots through the new mortality; Arthas finally drops.

nameUsage.arthasCallsSylvanas: Chapter23 gives him no meaningful intimate or political language for her; she is simply the armed obstacle that refuses to stay removed from his path.
nameUsage.sylvanasCallsArthas: By the final chamber fight his name refers to the wielder she intends to put down once the blade can finally be made to release him.

history.event: Their first on-page meeting happens under immediate tension: Arthas expects help or deference from Silvermoon and gets neither.
Sylvanas backs Jaina's grain warning with stripped route logic, making clear that this is not only a mage's theory but an operational read from the road itself.
Once Arthas's refusal is clear, Sylvanas ends the exchange cleanly and turns the quartet back toward proof rather than lingering inside his hierarchy.
Sylvanas briefs Arthas successfully in chapter 11, proving the warning has finally crossed from argument into action.
Uther’s warning and Sylvanas’s refusal to ride south mark the new limit of their alignment: she can activate Arthas, but she will not subordinate herself to his tempo.
Sylvanas gives Arthas the plague road in the language of routes, grain, and distribution rather than dread, and he acts on it at once.
Arthas explicitly leaves Sylvanas free to join either his column or Jaina’s lane; Sylvanas chooses the smaller run.
Sylvanas watches Arthas convert Jaina’s no into the Culling without trying to pretend the act still belongs inside hard necessity.
From the wall she reads the purge clearly enough to see both its tactical method and its moral rot, which permanently reclassifies Arthas in her internal file.
Arthas deliberately looks up at the wall after the purge, acknowledging witnesses rather than avoiding them.
Muradin's route briefing lets Sylvanas approach the cave still thinking in terms of intercepting Arthas. Seeing him with Frostmourne ends that assumption.
The cave fight confirms for Sylvanas that Arthas cannot be treated as a commander who made one more monstrous choice. He is now inseparable from the blade that altered the room around him.
Sylvanas leaves the encounter refusing the simpler story that the defeat belongs to bad partnership or bad execution. The enemy itself has changed class.
Sylvanas enters the Imperial Chamber already operating on the chapter-21 conclusion that Arthas cannot be fought as an ordinary commander or even as an ordinary monster.
She repeatedly buys Jaina time at direct bodily cost, confirming that the only workable lane against Arthas is a combined anti-blade sequence.
Sylvanas lands the final arrow after Jaina opens Frostmourne’s seam, ending the relationship as an accomplished kill rather than an unresolved vendetta.

trajectory: Formal first contact -> useful but dangerous military lever -> witness-based condemnation -> chapter 21's direct proof that Arthas has become the carrier of a larger and worse force. -> chapter 23’s completed throne-room execution.

stateHistory.currentState: Mutual professional recognition sharpened into strategic distrust. Arthas now takes Sylvanas seriously enough to move on her warning at once; Sylvanas now trusts his force more than his judgment.

Operational respect without trust. Arthas now trusts Sylvanas enough to offer her a free strategic choice; Sylvanas trusts his force enough to work around it and his judgment little enough to refuse the column.
Professional respect collapses into witness judgment. Sylvanas stops reading Arthas as a dangerous ally and starts reading him as the man who chose Stratholme.

Chapter 21 moves Sylvanas and Arthas from witness judgment to direct transformed confrontation. She finally meets him after Frostmourne and immediately revises the file: Arthas is no longer the primary tactical unit. The blade is.

Chapter 23 turns transformed confrontation into lethal completion. Sylvanas no longer revises the file from first contact; she executes it. Arthas remains the carrier of the sword’s force until Jaina opens the seam, and Sylvanas becomes the one who converts that new vulnerability into his end.
